# Transcrição: 06-Live Events_ Planning

- **Fonte:** 06-Live Events_ Planning
- **Duração:** 11:51
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hello, I am so thrilled to be here with you today because I get to talk about one of my favorite things, and that is planning an in-person mastermind event. Now, before I get into that, I should probably introduce myself. My name is Aubrey and I am the events director here at Mastermind. I have been with Mastermind for about five years now, and during that time I have gained valuable experience and insights into coordinating some incredible events for Dean. And I can't wait to share with you some of the strategies and tips I have learned in that time to really help you either enhance your current event or kick off your first mastermind. Together, we are going to walk through the secrets of crafting a successful, engaging, and transformative mastermind event. I can't wait to help you turn your vision into a reality and create an experience that truly inspires, connects, and empowers you and your clients. Now, there is plenty that goes into planning events, and at the beginning, I know it can feel incredibly overwhelming, but my goal by the end of these trainings is to show you how simple in-person events can be and all the pieces that go into them so that you can feel confident in your event planning journey. Now, we are going to break this up into about three different sections, planning your event, creating your event, and executing your event. In this section, we are going to be focusing on the planning process. Whether you are doing this by yourself, having a friend or a family member help you, or you have an entire team behind you, these strategies and tactics will support you in putting on a successful in-person event. But let's start at the beginning. Whenever a new event is brought to me, I always ask myself, what is the ultimate goal of this event? What do we want our attendees to take away from their experience? It's helpful to understand your end goal when you are building the milestones that will get you there. Having a clear 30,000-foot view of your event makes the smaller decisions that much more impactful. A trick to figure out the foundation of any event that I like to use is called the five W's of your event. The first, the who. Who is this event for? I really like to get into their mind. What are their aspirations? What are their challenges? If you aren't sure, I would recommend conducting a survey prior to the event to gain insights into where your audience is currently at and what they are needing. The second is the what. What specific outcomes do you aim to achieve during the event? What knowledge or skills should your attendees leave with by the end of the event? The third is the why. Why is this content significant to your audience? Why will they show up? But also, what's your why? Why are you hosting this Naster Mind? What's your goal? The fourth is the where. Where will you host this event? Where is going to perfectly align with your event's purpose and brand? I'll go over this more when I speak to selecting the perfect venue later on in this training. And lastly, the fifth is the when. When will you host the event? When will work for your attendees? You really want to guarantee max attendance.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

Once you start to understand these five W's of your event, you begin to build the foundation for your event. And then the logistics of planning become that much easier to start putting together because you understand where you want to go. It's like having a map on a road trip. Now, before we dive into the logistics and start making decisions in our planning process, there is one thing I want you to always keep in mind and keep coming back to. And that is keeping your brand in mind. You always want to make sure your decisions align with your brand identity, your values, the messaging, and the overall aesthetic to create a cohesive experience. For example, with Dean's branding, that often leads me to create events in upscale venues with healthy food options and speakers who are founded in the knowledge industry with a desire to help people get started. You wouldn't want to have a mastermind for healthy eating habits and then serve McDonald's for lunch. While every event is unique, there are several key logistics that you should always keep in mind when beginning to plan an event. And those can include budgeting, location, audio and visual requirements, catering, and room layout. But let's start with everyone's favorite, budgeting. It's essential to establish a budget early on and commit to it throughout your planning process. This is because it can be tempting to splurge, especially on your first event. But don't forget that the true value lies in the content that you provide. Everything else should either complement or enhance that core value. When planning a budget, it's easiest to start with a big picture in mind. What are you willing to invest in your event? I would consider the cost of attendance and how it aligns with your ticket pricing strategy, meaning the higher the ticket price, the higher the budget that you'll have. But also, you want to determine your priorities. From venue, catering, gifts or other elements, you may need to make trade-offs in one area to allocate resources to another if it's more important to you. To inform your budgeting decisions, you can look at past events you've organized or, if this is your first event, industry averages to gauge realistic costs. Check down the cost of everything imaginable for your event, from staff time to venue to logistics including AV, catering, gifts and more. It's always best to account for unexpected costs because events are unpredictable. Keep in mind that a budget is a dynamic tool. It will evolve as you gather more information and quotes from vendors. One of my biggest pieces of advice when it comes to budgeting is to always seek quotes from multiple vendors. Because remember, everything is negotiable. Now let's talk about your venue. When it comes to choosing your location, remember this. The venue is one of the first impressions your attendees will have of your event. Making it essential to select a venue that not only aligns with your brand, but also enhances the overall event atmosphere. Think of your venue as a canvas. Choosing the right canvas is essential to crafting a masterpiece.

## [06:01 - 09:02]

While you may consider hosting the event at your office or your home, exploring alternative venues can also add a unique touch to your event. If that's something you're looking to do, you can find so many cool locations in your area with a simple internet search on platforms like Google, Yelp or a platform that I personally like to use called eventup.com, where you can input the number of guests, the location, your budget and other details and you can browse through venue options to find the perfect batch for you. You'll want to ensure that the venue you choose provides enough space for your event to ensure the comfort of all attendees. Consider the layout to accommodate guests, speakers at the front and staff at the back. You will also want to make sure that there are windows and outdoor spaces to maintain energy levels throughout the day. Natural light and fresh air can have a significant impact on the overall energy and engagement at your event. If you can do an in-person walkthrough, I highly recommend that you do. This will give you more insight into how the event will look in this space, but if it's not an option, most venues nowadays have a 360 walkthrough that they can provide. Remember what I said earlier, everything is negotiable. Ask questions to the venue, negotiate your terms. The key is to exude confidence in the vision of your event, even if it's your first event. They don't need to know that. When you have a strong foundation and your focus is on your attendees' experience, you will have complete confidence in how you want your event to look. A bonus tip, if your event has the potential to become reoccurring, mention it to your venue. This can help build a strong relationship with the venue that can lead to additional benefits and discounts down the road. When you establish a positive rapport with the venue staff, it can pave the way for smoother planning processes and enhanced event experiences in the future. The next logistic to keep in mind is your audio and visual needs, or commonly known as AV. Your AV needs will be very dependent on the nature and scale of your event. For more casual events, you may not require extensive AV equipment. For more intensive events, you may consider bringing in items such as projectors, white boards, microphones, cameras, and other items to support your mastermind. If you're doing it at a hotel or a similar venue, they usually can provide this in your initial contract, so make sure to ask. One of the most overlooked AV aspects in events is photography. Capturing high-quality images during your event serves multiple purposes. Not only do these photos provide valuable promotional material for your next event, but they also enable you to be able to share professional images on social media and with your attendees, who can also share on their social media. Another key logistic of any event that most people don't think matters but truly leaves a lasting impression on your guests is catering. If you're hosting your event at a hotel or a similar venue, they typically will have a food and beverage minimum that you will need to meet. Otherwise, you have the option to work with caterers or restaurants to create your own menu for the event. Typically, for full-day events, I like to provide a light breakfast that will help kickstart

## [09:02 - 11:51]

the day, a healthy lunch that energizes and fuels your attendees, afternoon snacks to keep everybody's energy levels up, and a continuous supply of coffee, tea, and water to make sure everyone stays refreshed and hydrated. People always remember the food for the good, the bad, and the ugly, so don't underestimate the impact that great food can have on the success of your event. One last logistic that can play a significant role in shaping the dynamics and interactions among your attendees is the room layout. Customizing the room setup to your group and the nature of the event can either enhance or take away from the collaboration, communication, and overall engagement at the event. For smaller groups, a U-table setup is a great option for masterminds and can be highly effective in fostering a sense of intimacy in the room and encourage collaboration, communication, for the entire group. This is probably what most of you will be doing at first, but for those of you who have a bigger group, smaller roundtables to create groups works best. This helps keep the room intimate even when there are a larger amount of people and also allows for more opportunities for discussions to happen. It's essential to think through the composition of each table group. Are they at the same level of business? Are they struggling with the same thing? Will they get along? A bad table can really take away from the experience of your attendees, so make sure to be mindful about your groups if you choose this option. You'll want to ensure that the front area has enough room and is designated for your speakers as well. Typically, I recommend having a small, high-top table for notes, water, and other essentials, a comfortable chair for speakers, even though they might not use it, but it's nice to have just in case. And anything else that they would need, a whiteboard, a flip chart, or anything else they'll need to present their slides. You'll also want to think through where you'll be placing your food and drink if that's something you're providing. Strategically placing these in either the back or the side of the room can help minimize distraction during the event. This also ensures attendees can still have access to refreshments without missing the event. If it makes sense for your event, incorporating signage and branding elements into the room can reinforce the theme or messaging of your event. Consider displaying your logo on screens or providing branded materials to create a cohesive visual identity. These elements can not only enhance the ambiance of the room, but also provide opportunities for attendees to capture photos that display your brand. Thinking through these logistics will help you lay the foundation for your event, understand what you want to accomplish, and the details that go into planning and a successful event. Congratulations! You are now one step closer to hosting your in-person mastermind. In the next training, we're going to breathe life into this vision that you've started to plan and bring the 5 Ws of your event to fruition through agendas, speakers, event assets, and more. I'll see you over there.
